Quick search

Physics Background¶

Radar Cross Section¶

Radar Cross Section (RCS) is a measure of how detectable an object is by radar. It is defined as the equivalent area that would produce the same scattered power as an isotropic scatterer:

\[\sigma = \lim_{R\to\infty} 4\pi R^2 \frac{|\mathbf{E}_\text{scat}|^2}{|\mathbf{E}_\text{inc}|^2}\]

RCS is typically expressed in dBsm (decibels relative to one square meter):

\[\sigma_\text{dBsm} = 10 \log_{10}(\sigma)\]

Coordinate System¶

Observation angles are defined in the standard spherical coordinate system:

  • \(\theta\) (theta): elevation angle from \(+Z\) axis (0\(^\circ\) to 180\(^\circ\))

  • \(\phi\) (phi): azimuth angle around \(Z\) axis (0\(^\circ\) to 360\(^\circ\))

    +Z (incident wave)
    |
    |
    |    theta
    |
    |
    O---------> X
   /
  / phi
 /
Y

Physical Optics (PO)¶

Physical Optics approximates scattering from PEC targets using the tangent-plane approximation. The equivalent surface current is:

\[\mathbf{J}_s = 2\; \hat{n} \times \mathbf{H}_\text{inc}\]

where \(\hat{n}\) is the surface normal and \(\mathbf{H}_\text{inc}\) is the incident magnetic field. The scattered far-field is:

\[\mathbf{E}_\text{scat} = \frac{jk}{4\pi} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbf{J}_{s,i} \, e^{jk\hat{r}\cdot\mathbf{r}'_i} \Delta A_i\]

where:

  • \(k = 2\pi/\lambda\) is the wavenumber

  • \(\hat{r}\) is the observation direction

  • \(\mathbf{r}'_i\) is the centroid of triangle \(i\)

  • \(\Delta A_i\) is the area of triangle \(i\)

PO provides full angular coverage but ignores edge diffraction and multiple reflections.

Shooting & Bouncing Rays (SBR)¶

SBR traces rays from a launch plane toward the target. Each ray represents a tube of the incident plane wave. The ray-object intersection is found using the BVH, and the scattered field is accumulated:

\[\mathbf{E}_\text{scat} = \sum_\text{rays} \mathbf{J}_s \, e^{jk(\hat{r} \cdot \mathbf{r}')} \Delta A\]

SBR captures multiple reflections (ray bouncing) but only produces backscatter contributions. It is complementary to PO for complex targets.

BVH Acceleration¶

The Bounding Volume Hierarchy (BVH) accelerates ray-triangle intersection queries using SAH (Surface Area Heuristic) binning. Each BVH node stores an AABB; leaves contain up to 4 triangles. The Möller-Trumbore algorithm is used for per-triangle intersection.

Polarization¶

RCS is computed for two linear polarizations:

  • VV: Vertical transmit, vertical receive. The incident field is \(\mathbf{E}_\text{inc} = \hat{\mathbf{e}}_\theta\) and the scattered field is projected onto \(\hat{\mathbf{e}}_\theta\).

  • HH: Horizontal transmit, horizontal receive. The scattered field is projected onto \(\hat{\mathbf{e}}_\phi\).

Monostatic vs Bistatic¶

  • Monostatic (default): The observation direction is the negative of the incident direction. The incident wave comes from \(+Z\) and the receiver is co-located with the transmitter.

  • Bistatic: The incident wave direction is fixed (\(\theta_\text{inc}\), \(\phi_\text{inc}\)) and the scattered field is computed at all observation angles.
